body: This is a tracking issue for planned `vim.pack` improvements. Please do not leave comments in this issue.

comment_data.body:
> @echasnovski Is there any plan to create a minimal ui for viewing installed plugins via vim.pack? Users can interact with the vim.pack api through this simple ui or through the user commands like in [#34764](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/34764)

Confirmation buffer with in-process LSP is what is intended for this purpose. Especially after #34919 and lockfile support.

An update on the plans for the 0.12 release:

- ~~Lockfile support (#35827).~~ Done
- ~~Try hard to convince merging code actions in confirmation buffer PR (#34919).~~ Done. Didn't require that much convincing eventually.
- ~~More strict `vim.pack.add`. Like enforcing `source` (reclone) and `version` (recheckout) if they changed (#34771). Make sure to keep #36131 in mind.~~ Done via #36435. See [this comment](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/34771#issuecomment-3543354672) for more details.
- ~~Dedicated `:checkhealth` that checks correct lockfile data and outdated tags (#34777).~~ Done
- ~~Maybe try to make "revert from latest update" more straightforward (#36131).~~ Done
- ~~Maybe force install all plugins from lockfile at once (for more complete lazy loading experience, #34771).~~ Done.
- ~~Maybe `version=false` support for easier freeze.~~ Having this be "just easier freeze" seems to much (as setting to a commit/tag seems to be easy enough now with both `vim.pack.get()` and `vim.pack.update('plug-name', { offline = true })`). One approach where it can be more useful is by marking currently managed plugin as unmanaged. See #37727 for more thoughts on the subject.
- ~~Maybe try resolving "local plugins" (#34765).~~ My best attempt is in #37727. Although I still am not really fond of the idea of having such support in the first place.
- ~~Explore lazy loading generalized helpers as part of `vim.func`.~~ Not quite `vim.func`, but I think #37618 is a reasonable approach here.

Hey 👋 

I dunno where to put this comment best. I hope this isn’t too much noise here. Else I’ll create a dedicated issue for it.

Currently, vim.pack only supports a single dedicated package (as defined by `vim.pack-directory`). And unfortunately it makes all plugins within this package `opt`ional (into the `opt` folder). However, plenty of plugins I would like be installed as a `start` plugin (into the `start` folder). So they get loaded automatically during start, based on the regular startup procedure. No fuzz to load them via my configuration explicitly every time. Plain NeoVim.

I must admit I’m a little confused about the approach that vim.pack takes, by installing all plugins as optionally, but loads them “immediately” afterwards (using `vim.cmd.packadd`). You must actually opt out via a custom `load` function. A little awkward to me.

My current approach is to avoid the whole plugin manager noise during startup. In exact I don’t call any plugin manager functionality in any automatically loaded file. Instead I put these calls into dedicated files in the runtime path where NeoVim ignores them per default. If I wanna have any plugins become installed or updated, I can source them on demand via `vim.cmd.runtime({ 'START plugin_specification.lua', bang = true })` (the file name is my own convention).
I have my own lazy loading functionality in place to load optional plugins where meaningful. However, I rely also on simple, plain, strait forward `start` plugins.

Are there any plans to support start plugins as well as optional plugins for the same core package managed via vim.pack?

Thank you!

> I dunno where to put this comment best. I hope this isn’t too much noise here. Else I’ll create a dedicated issue for it.

This is a tracking issue. You're better off making a dedicated issue. Comments here will likely just get marked as off-topic.

> I must admit I’m a little confused about the approach that vim.pack takes, by installing all plugins as optionally, but loads them “immediately” afterwards (using `vim.cmd.packadd`). You must actually opt out via a custom `load` function. A little awkward to me.

Plugins can be loaded at startup by calling `vim.pack.add` at startup. They are "optional" because there is no way to know when they might be added (e.g. you might `vim.pack.add` some plugins lazily). This makes your config idempotent.

> My current approach is to avoid the whole plugin manager noise during startup. In exact I don’t call any plugin manager functionality in any automatically loaded file.

I'm not sure I'm seeing the benefit of doing things this way. If you don't call any package manager functionality, you can't guarantee that a plugin is installed, and configuring the plugin becomes much harder.

> Are there any plans to support start plugins as well as optional plugins for the same core package managed via vim.pack?

Have you considered just cloning the git repositories of the plugins you want into `start/`, and not using `vim.pack` for those?

As far as I'm aware, most third-party package managers work the same way as `vim.pack`, where they do not load plugins that are not explicitly declared in code. For instance, `lazy.nvim` and `mini.deps` both behave this way.

Using `opt/` was an intentional design decision so that disabling plugins is a single step: just comment-out the line in your config. That doesn't work with `start/`. 

> I don’t call any plugin manager functionality in any automatically loaded file

Then you don't need `vim.pack` ...

Thank you very much for all the fast and kind replies! 🙏

I understand much better now, that this is intentional design. And that for my current mindset, vim.pack does not fit.

I'll follow your impressive work and maybe I'll change my mind.

With #37727 and #37618 in place, I think I did my best to cover all my promises from [this comment](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/34763#issuecomment-3330305488) before 0.12 release.

Other still open issues for `vim.pack` improvements are either:
- Something I'd add, but they are blocked as unwanted: #34764, #34768, #34772, #36078.
- Out of my depth: #34775, #26427, #36066.
- Planned until after couple of months will have passed form 0.12 release (as it will require a lot of discussions): #34778.

https://github.com/folke/lazy.nvim/discussions/2139
-> I have added some more comments trying to repeat the most
important points below.

Neovim kinda fixed the Viml/Perl/Ruby/PHP plugin mess.

And the plugin manager mess (eg nix brew C dependencies cargo npm gem ..)
requires thinking out of the box and escaping it.

While NeoVim probably can't afford to fix the mess for 50+ languages
maybe plugin configurations can be designed in a way making such implementation
possible in the future. Many tools like Emacs went through the many phases of
the many addon managers of yeah .. Maybe its time to get it more right ?

vim-addon-manager once introduced vim-addon-info.json.
lazy.nvim allows to copy paste mappings (commands ?) to load plugins lazily?
But what should apt install (same for nix) where is the recommended default ?

Thus if a plugin manager (within nix, brew, apt, gentoo, chocolately, nvim.lazy,
neovim, buck2, ...) would implement a common interface the implementation could
be switched. So whether installing a plugin is actually downloading, mounting,
checking it out, installing from system or git cloning it .. would be an
implementation detail ?

Should it include LSP ? Should it include other universes like blender, krita,
... ?

So from my point of view the perfect declaration would look like this

```
{
  if python use micromamba env X
  unless fontforge, then try python3-xxx (using apt) if on debian/ubuntu
  cause stupid C dependencies hard to compile yourself :-/
  Even a simple requirement eventually can totally flip the installation
  strategy in the worst case :(

  pool = [
    ubuntu-xx-native
    my-own-plugin-list
  ]

  packages = solved(pool)

  packages.krita
  packages.krita.plugin1
  packages.tsgo
  packages.plugin

  neovim {
    plugins = { 
      // now this is nvim.lazy like configuration ..
      // eg configuring mappings and commands and overwrites
      // and configs or merging you personal changes
      // etc
      // This is probably the part you think about
      // but can you mock commands ? Thus once a command is run
      // load the plugin and redefine the command and rerun it ?
  }}

  -- ensure they are on disk so I can change them
  -- and fix some bugs
  hack-on = [
    packages.krita.plugin1
    packages.tsgo
    packages.plugin
  ]

}
```
Additionally something like VAMActivate or require('lazy').load({...}) to lazily install and or load something later seems sane ? So dependencies could be >otionally< or >to be installed as need lazily< or marked as such, too.

Suddenly either a lua or an external or a nix like implementation
could solve install prepare binaries or whatever ..
And defining and patching the >pool< allows to inject patches.
So buck2 or brew or chocolately or cmake or whatever could be targeted.

What other way exists to kinda find a solution for
  - your GPU (eg old nvidia)
  - python version
  - gcc compiler / ABI
  - plugins
  - editor
  - targeting older debugger only implemented by neovim-lua-php-xdebug < 0.8
  - ...

than throwing all into a bag running a solver hoping there is a solution ?

Harmony OS vs Fuchsia they even kinda mmap packages and download while using
And Harmony OS has prepackaged packages allowing to use them as services no
matter what arch or device just call by name and it will be provided.

And your custom >pool< to be used for the solver is very important because
eg Haskell's cabal files often work with newer GCs but the dependencies haven't
been updated because maintainer is busy - os you could easily merge in some
well mantained patches to make things work till upstream is fixed.

And then all tools and libraries and editors could eventually join.

If this wasn't a major regular headache to me I would not talk about it :)

In the past when writing vim-addon-manager I had better things todo.
But I felt the need to distribute plugins and their dependencies.
Now vundle and others appeared. And all README's on github kinda turned
into redundant information how to install those 20 lines of VIML using the 5+
different packge managers :) We have AI today - yet - I'd love to see some true
future proof solutions.

Side notes: finding solutions to dependency hell is a SAT issue.
There are new CPP solvers with nicer error messages (eg used by micromamba and
others) today. Yet Haskell solved it by defining a platform. Meaning X packages
get released and maintained together reducing complexity. So the hell turned into
ghc bigger or smaller verion 2.0 rather than 5-10 core module combinations.

About versioning: Eg Java had the issue if you want to support sql-1.0 and sql-2.0
its hard to do. So if you have breaking changes and move the highest number
into the same sql1-0 sql2-0 the problem is gone cause you can instal both as if
they were different packges (they actually eventually are).

Eg compare with the Python hell of Zarr archives where 3 wasn't compatible
to 2 but all imports where the same and 3 then implemenetd *SOME* read only
support of version 2 and .. yeah - better have import zarr2 or import zarr3 and
you know what you're dealing with at all times ?

Sry for adding some learnings from the past so that history doesn't repeat ? ..
:)

Thus summing up: Getting plugins and dependencies right would fix much more.
Also providing C lib locations to npm installations on Nixos linux .. :/
Of course this is just my view. So feel free to ignore. Most of the tool solve one problem but cause some headache somewhere else. Like installing something with docker (specific mysql) is easier than using nixpkgs.  What's the point of nixos/nixpkgs if snap/docker are the solutions which always work ? And the concept shown here would kinda allow all parties with different goals to collaborate as much as possible. I think the specification and a reusable interface is even more important than the implementation. With a LSP like API a lua (neovim internal) or an external solution could be used expanding scope and view on the dependency world.

https://github.com/opensuse/libsolv is the SAT solver which is used by many managers today. And there is xmake and many others kinda duplicating some of dependency hell in some way or the other.

milestone.description: Low priority items. We plan to work on this but don't have a target date.
milestone.title: backlog

title: `vim.pack` improvements

type.name: Enhancement
